beats.text: Here's a five-twelve by five-twelve grid of tiny squares — that's our grayscale image.
Count them: five-twelve times five-twelve equals two-hundred-sixty-two-thousand-one-hundred-forty-four pixels total.

Each pixel holds one number — zero for black, two-fifty-five for white, everything in between is a shade of gray. Stack all of them into a single column vector in R two-sixty-two-thousand-one-hundred-forty-four.
Now a label appears: eight bits per pixel. One byte. That's the storage cost for every single entry.

Switch to a colour image — same grid, but now each pixel splits into three entries: red, green, blue channels. The vector triples in size, landing in R seven-hundred-eighty-six-thousand-four-hundred-thirty-two.
